nameMeanings.name: Maidwell
nameMeanings.attribution: Dictionary of Family Names in Britain and Ireland © University of the West of England 2016

variants.name: Madewell
meanings: English: variant of Maidwell, a habitational name from a place in Northamptonshire named Maidwell, from Old English mægth ‘maiden’ + wella ‘well, spring, stream’.

variants.name: Madle
meanings: 1 Norman, English: nickname from Old French, Middle English masle, madle (also Middle English male), ‘a man, an adult’ or adjectivally ‘male, masculine’. William le Masle (1280), le Madle (1303), or le Male (1305) has left his name in Marles Farm in Epping Upland (Essex).
2 English: perhaps sometimes a variant of Maidwell , with loss of -w- in the unstressed syllable.

nameMeanings.name: Alan
nameMeanings.attribution: Dictionary of First Names © Patrick Hanks and Flavia Hodges 1990, 2003, 2006.
meanings: Of Celtic origin and uncertain derivation (possibly a diminutive of a word meaning ‘rock’). It was introduced into England by Breton followers of William the Conqueror , most notably Alan , Earl of Brittany, who was rewarded for his services with vast estates in the newly conquered kingdom. In Britain the variants Allan and Allen are considerably less frequent, and generally represent transferred uses of surname forms, whereas in America all three forms of the name are approximately equally common. See also Alun .

historicEvents.title: Magna Carta
historicEvents.description: King John of England agreed to a charter of rights called the Magna Carta. The document was originally drafted by the Archbishop of Canterbury to establish peace between King John and a group of rebel barons. The Magna Carta was completed in 1215 and is now considered to be one of the greatest constitutional documents of all time.

historicEvents.title: France Declares War on England
historicEvents.description: France declared war on England as Louis VIII of France attacked Poitou and northern Gascony.

en.lifeSummary: Alan Maidwell ,Sheriff of Northamptonshire 1233-39 was born in England as the son of Simon De Maidwell and Agnes  or Anneys Chesney or Cheney. He had at least 2 sons. He died in 1250, in Ellesborough, Buckinghamshire, England.
